title: Entanglement generation in a two-body Schrödinger--Newton model
authors: Marcin Płodzień, Julia Osęka-Lenart, Maciej Lewenstein, Michał Eckstein
abstract: The Schrödinger--Newton (SN) equation provides a semiclassical framework for the evolution of self-gravitating of massive quantum systems. We propose a two-body Schrödinger--Newton model that separates local nonlinear self-localization from the nonseparable Newtonian pair potential. Analytically, we show that the nonlinear self-field preserves the Schmidt spectrum, whereas direct entanglement generation arises from the nonseparable pair potential. Using numerical simulations in a regularized one-dimensional geometry, we find that entanglement generation depends sensitively on the initial spatial configuration and on the mass ratio. Highly localized, self-bound wavepackets experience minimal entanglement growth during scattering. Spatial delocalization and kinetic dispersion broaden the interaction region, amplifying the entangling power of the pair potential and exciting higher-order spatial modes. For dispersive Gaussian initial states, mass asymmetry shatters the lighter particle, producing Wigner negativity and rapid entanglement growth, whereas stationary SN profiles strongly suppress this effect. Stationary SN profiles isolate the bare pair-potential contribution; dispersive Gaussian initial states inflate it.
summary: This paper explores how entanglement arises in a two-body system governed by the Schrödinger-Newton equation. It demonstrates that entanglement is driven by the inter-particle pair potential rather than self-interaction, and that spatial dispersion and mass asymmetry significantly enhance this entanglement generation.
main_problem: Investigating the mechanism of entanglement generation in a two-body Schrödinger-Newton model and distinguishing between the effects of nonlinear self-localization and nonseparable Newtonian pair potentials.
main_result: The authors show that while nonlinear self-fields preserve the Schmidt spectrum, entanglement is driven specifically by the nonseparable pair potential, with growth being amplified by spatial delocalization and mass asymmetry.
method: Analytical derivation of the Schmidt spectrum evolution and numerical simulations using second-order Strang splitting and FFT-based potential calculations in a 1D regularized geometry.
model_or_system: A two-body Schrödinger-Newton model featuring both local nonlinear self-gravitational potentials and a nonseparable Newtonian pair potential between two particles.
key_observables: von Neumann entropy, Schmidt eigenvalues, Wigner function negativity, Linear entropy, and Participation Ratio.
important_parameters: Self-gravitational coupling (kappa), pair-gravitational coupling (gamma), mass ratio (mu1/mu2), and regularization scale (epsilon).
main_assumptions: The use of a 1D regularized geometry with a softened kernel to avoid singularities; the derivation of the mean-field limit assumes a product-state (Hartree) ansatz.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate collision dynamics of Gaussian vs. stationary profiles, the evolution of joint and marginal densities, Wigner function fragmentation, and the impact of mass asymmetry on entanglement growth.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the two-body SN model, provides an analytical proof regarding the preservation of the Schmidt spectrum by self-fields, presents numerical results on entanglement growth for various initial states and mass ratios, and concludes with a variational derivation of the mean-field (Hartree) limit.
why_interesting: It provides fundamental insights into how semiclassical gravity-like interactions can generate quantum correlations and explores the boundary between mean-field dynamics and entanglement-rich many-body evolution.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Collision dynamics for the localized product state ΨI (Eq. 32) using Gaussian wavepackets. The snapshots are taken at t = 0, 10, 20, 30, 40. The panel shows (top row) the joint probability density |Ψ(x1, x2)|2; (middle row) single-particle marginal densities ρ1(x) (blue) and ρ2(x) (red); (bottom row) the relative-coordinate Wigner function Wrel. Parameters: R0 = 6, κ = γ = 1, σ0 = 1.

Figure 2: Collision dynamics for the localized product state ΨI (Eq. 32) using stationary SN profiles. The snapshots are taken at t = 0, 10, 20, 30, 40. The panel shows (top row) the joint probability density |Ψ(x1, x2)|2; (middle row) single-particle marginal densities ρ1(x) (blue) and ρ2(x) (red); (bottom row) the relative-coordinate Wigner function Wrel. Parameters: R0 = 6, κ = γ = 1; the imaginary-time seed width is σ0 = 1.

Figure 3: Evolution of the delocalized product state ΨII (Eq. 33) using stationary SN profiles. Despite being initialized from stationary profiles, the LL/RR and LR/RL components acquire different phases and accelerations under the pair potential, producing SvN ≈1.5. The panel shows (top row) the joint probability density |Ψ(x1, x2)|2; (middle row) single-particle marginal densities ρ1(x) and ρ2(x); (bottom row) the relative-coordinate Wigner function Wrel, with snapshots at t = 0, 10, 20, 30, 40. Parameters: R0 = 6, κ = γ = 1; the imaginary-time seed width is σ0 = 1.

Figure 4: Evolution of the correlated superposition state ΨIII (Eq. 34) using stationary SN profiles. The entanglement entropy oscillates around SvN ≈1.28, reflecting deformation of the two same-side components under the common self-field and pair attraction. The panel shows (top row) the joint probability density |Ψ(x1, x2)|2; (middle row) single-particle marginal densities ρ1(x) and ρ2(x); (bottom row) the relative-coordinate Wigner function Wrel, with snapshots at t = 0, 10, 20, 30, 40. Parameters: R0 = 6, κ = γ = 1; the imaginary-time seed width is σ0 = 1.

Figure 5: Evolution of the anticorrelated superposition state ΨIV (Eq. 35) using stationary SN profiles. The mutual gravitational attraction drives the disjoint spatial components together, increasing the entanglement entropy to SvN ≈1.67. The panel shows (top row) the joint probability density |Ψ(x1, x2)|2; (middle row) single-particle marginal densities ρ1(x) and ρ2(x); (bottom row) the relative-coordinate Wigner function Wrel, with snapshots at t = 0, 10, 20, 30, 40. Parameters: R0 = 6, κ = γ = 1; the imaginary-time seed width is σ0 = 1.
